title: Your First Claim Analysis: Is This Argument Any Good?
description: Take a claim from today's news or a conversation and subject it to rigorous critical analysis. This session builds the core skills of distinguishing claims from facts, evaluating evidence, and detecting fallacies.

steps.instruction: Find a claim someone has made (a news headline, social media post, or opinion from a conversation). Read about claims vs facts and opinions: is this claim a fact, an opinion, or something in between?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can categorize the claim and explain why — what evidence would be needed to verify it as factual?
steps.hint: A fact is verifiable with evidence; an opinion expresses a preference or judgment; many claims mix both

steps.instruction: Look for evidence supporting the claim. Where does the evidence come from? Who created it? With what purpose? Read about sourcing and evidence quality: what makes evidence strong or weak?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify the source of evidence and apply at least two criteria for assessing its reliability (expertise, independence, date, transparency of methods)
steps.hint: Ask: Is this source an expert? Is it independent of the claim-maker? Is the evidence primary or secondhand?

steps.instruction: Read about common logical fallacies. Does the argument use ad hominem, straw man, false dichotomy, or appeal to authority? Identify any you find (even if you have to look hard).
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify at least one potential fallacy in the argument or confirm none are present and explain your reasoning
steps.hint: False dichotomy is the most common — 'either X or Y' when more options exist

steps.instruction: Construct the strongest possible counterargument to the claim. Read about charitable interpretation: why should you steelman opposing views?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have articulated a genuine counterargument (not a strawman) and explained why steelmanning opposing views produces stronger thinking
steps.hint: A steelman states the opposition's best case, not their worst — it strengthens your analysis by testing your position against real objections

steps.instruction: Reflect on your own reaction to the claim. Did you agree quickly? Disagree without reading? Read about confirmation bias and intellectual humility: how might your prior beliefs have colored your analysis?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify at least one way your existing beliefs may have affected how you evaluated this claim, and describe what truly neutral analysis would require
steps.hint: We all have confirmation bias — the goal is not to eliminate it but to notice when it is operating
